message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in current code (6 passes hardened). confirmed=true only if real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative/by-design).
J-001: Strict UTF-8 decode in scrubber fails OPEN: one invalid byte routes the whole transcript to verbatim copy, re-leaking secrets/PII
File: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113
VERIFIED. _copy_scrubbed() does `try: text = raw.decode('utf-8') except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw`. A single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the ENTIRE file to the verbatim branch, copied byte-for-byte with no scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1/non-UTF-8 file reads, truncated multibyte from killed processes) inside JSONL string values, so this is a common condition. Pre-CV-O-1 the .jsonl path decoded with errors='replace' and was ALWAYS scrubbed; the CV-O-1 fix turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one precisely for the files most likely to carry raw dumped data. This is the new code being the defect — the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path, no credential-shaped secret) now silently fails for affected files.
PoC: src.write_bytes(b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'); raw=src.read_bytes(); try: data=_scrub(raw.decode('utf-8')).encode() except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw  ->  b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'REDACTED' in data == False. Under pre-CV-O-1 code the same file was decoded errors='replace' and fully scrubbed.
Fix: Decode for scrubbing with errors='replace' (or 'backslashreplace'/'surrogatepass') so a stray byte never disables redaction: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')`. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (`if b'\x00' in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must come out scrubbed.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)

content.text: The code matches the finding exactly. Lines 108-113 decode strictly with UTF-8, and on `UnicodeDecodeError`, route the entire file to verbatim copy with no scrubbing. Let me verify the PoC behavior and confirm the patterns would actually catch these secrets when scrubbing works.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:

content.text: Now let me reproduce the PoC to confirm the fail-open behavior.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c '
import sys; sys.argv=["x"]
import importlib.util
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("ct", "scripts/capture_transcripts.py")
m = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(m)

raw = b"{\"out\":\"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x\"}"
try:
    text = raw.decode("utf-8")
    data = m._scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
    branch="scrub"
except UnicodeDecodeError:
    data = raw
    branch="verbatim"
print("branch:", branch)
print("leaked token:", b"[REDACTED]" in data)
print("leaked user:", b"[REDACTED]" in data)
print("has REDACTED:", b"REDACTED" in data)

# compare: if it were valid utf-8 (errors=replace) it would scrub
text2 = raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
data2 = m._scrub(text2).encode("utf-8")
print("--- with errors=replace ---")
print("leaked token:", b"[REDACTED]" in data2)
print("leaked user:", b"[REDACTED]" in data2)
print("has REDACTED:", b"REDACTED" in data2)
'

content.content:
branch: verbatim
leaked token: True
leaked user: True
has REDACTED: False
--- with errors=replace ---
leaked token: False
leaked user: False
has REDACTED: True

content.text:
Reproduced exactly. The PoC confirms: with a single invalid byte (`\x80`), the entire file routes to the verbatim branch and the secret token + operator username leak with no redaction. With `errors='replace'`, the same content is fully scrubbed.

Let me confirm the practical relevance — whether non-UTF-8 bytes plausibly occur in these transcripts. The finding argues Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout. This is a real condition: tool outputs (file reads, hexdumps, latin-1 content, truncated multibyte) are embedded as JSON string values in the JSONL, and a single non-UTF-8 byte anywhere disables scrubbing for the whole file.

Assessment:
- The code is exactly as described (lines 108-113).
- The fail-open behavior is real and reproducible.
- This is a regression introduced by the CV-O-1 change — the prior `.jsonl` path used `errors='replace'` and always scrubbed; the new strict-decode-then-verbatim logic turned an always-redact path into fail-open precisely for files likely to carry raw dumped data.
- It defeats the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no credential-shaped secrets, no operator PII) for affected files committed to the repo.
- The fix is sound: decode with `errors='replace'` for scrubbing, and if a binary path is still desired, gate it on a real binary signal (`b'\x00' in raw`) rather than UTF-8 validity.

This is a true production-readiness problem. Severity: the impact is leaking credential-shaped secrets and operator PII into committed/published provenance, but the trigger requires an invalid byte in a transcript and the README already warns transcripts may contain PII and shouldn't be published without review. The defense-in-depth secret redaction is the load-bearing guarantee being silently broken. I assess this as high.

input.reasoning: Confirmed against actual code at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113. _copy_scrubbed() does `try: text = raw.decode("utf-8") except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (else: scrub). A single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the ENTIRE file to the verbatim branch, copied byte-for-byte with no redaction. Reproduced the PoC: input `b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'` -> branch=verbatim, [REDACTED] token LEAKED, operator username '[REDACTED]' LEAKED, no REDACTED present. With errors='replace' the identical content is fully scrubbed (token redacted, username redacted, REDACTED present). This is a real regression: the comment at lines 104-106 documents that CV-O-1 broadened scrubbing to all files because the old gate copied .json RAW; but the new strict-decode-then-verbatim classifier reintroduces a fail-open path precisely for the files most likely to embed raw subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1/non-UTF-8 file content, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL string values — exactly the content carrying dumped secrets/PII. UTF-8 invalidity != binary, so the classification is wrong. It silently defeats the load-bearing privacy guarantee (no credential-shaped secrets, no operator email/username/path) for affected committed provenance files. Fix is sound and low-risk: decode with errors='replace' for scrubbing, and gate any verbatim path on a real binary signal (b'\x00' in raw) instead of UTF-8 validity; add a regression test for a .jsonl containing both a secret and an invalid byte. Not by-design and not speculative — reproducible. Severity high rather than critical because the trigger requires an invalid byte to be present and the README already cautions that transcripts may contain PII and should be reviewed before any public release; the broken guarantee is the defense-in-depth redaction layer.

content.text: Confirmed J-001 as a true high-severity production-readiness problem. The fail-open strict UTF-8 decode at `scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113` was reproduced exactly: a single invalid byte routes the whole transcript to verbatim copy, leaking the credential token and operator username with no redaction, while `errors='replace'` scrubs the identical content correctly.
